images.caption: Figure 1:(a)Existing human animation methods primarily suffer from two types of drift: low-level quality degradation and high-level identity change.(b)Our method alleviates both issues, achieving stable animation. The bottom row provides zoomed-in views of the facial region and the background.

Figure 2:Illustration of errors in long-range animation videos.We visualize (a) VAE round-trip reconstructions and (b) DiT self-attention maps across video chunks at different lengths. In each attention map, the 1stcol.(highlighted in red) shows how video tokens attend to the global reference frame.Although most tokens correctly attend to this reference (i.e., forming an attention sink[42,46]), the generated video still exhibits progressive degradation over time, failing to anchor the long-horizon generation.The 2ndcol.shows attention to the last frame of the preceding chunk.

Figure 3:Overview of EverAnimate.Train:from a context chunkV(1)V^{(1)}, we extract motion/identity memoriesMid/motM_{\mathrm{id}/\mathrm{mot}}and train the model to generate the next chunkV(2)V^{(2)}with restorative flow matching.Test:We roll out chunk-by-chunk in the latent space without decoding frames between chunks.
Figure 5:RFM illustration.Comparison between our RFM and the standard FM baseline.

Figure 6:Effects of the reschedule.Comparison of the training stability with and without reschedulingλ​(t)\lambda{(t)}, i.e., Eq. (9) vs. Eq. (11). Our rescheduling design, shown in orange, can stabilize training.
Figure 7:Qualitative comparison with state-of-the-art methods. See supplementary material for videos. The bottom-right region is cropped according to the face regions in the ground-truth videos.
